subtopic: Febrile young infant - septic workup criteria
stem: A 6-week-old, ex-39-week, formula-fed, vaccinated-to-age boy is brought to ED by his mother after a measured rectal temperature of 38.4 degC at home this morning. He has fed slightly less than usual over the last 6 hours but has had wet nappies, no vomiting, no diarrhoea, no rash and no cough. There were no perinatal risk factors: maternal GBS was negative at 36 weeks, membranes were ruptured for 4 hours, and he was discharged home day 2 with no antibiotics. He was previously well. On arrival he is alert, settles to feed, capillary refill is 2 seconds centrally, perfusion is normal, and he has no focus on examination including a clear chest, soft non-tender abdomen, no bulging fontanelle, and a clean umbilicus. Recorded temperature in ED is 38.2 degC. Initial bloods show WCC 9.8 x10^9/L, neutrophils 5.2, lymphocytes 3.6, CRP 8 mg/L. Urinalysis dip on a clean-catch sample shows nitrites negative, leucocytes negative. The ED registrar is considering whether a lumbar puncture is required.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.2 degC, HR 148 bpm, RR 42, SpO2 99% RA, cap refill 2 s.
data_table.Examination: Alert, feeds well. No rash. Fontanelle flat. Chest clear. Abdomen soft. Umbilicus clean.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 9.8, neutrophils 5.2, lymphocytes 3.6, CRP 8 mg/L. Urine dip negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with paracetamol and 24-hour open review by the GP
options.rationale: All febrile infants under 3 months require investigation; outpatient management without workup is not acceptable regardless of how well they appear (RCH CPG Febrile child; NICE NG143).

options.text: Perform blood cultures, urine culture (catheter or SPA) and lumbar puncture, then commence IV benzylpenicillin and gentamicin
options.rationale: Infants under 8 weeks with fever require full septic workup including CSF regardless of clinical appearance because the negative predictive value of low-risk criteria is insufficient to exclude bacterial meningitis at this age. Empirical IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin covers GBS, E. coli and Listeria (RCH CPG Febrile child; Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

options.text: Send blood and urine cultures only and observe without antibiotics pending results
options.rationale: Selective workup (omitting LP) is only considered in well infants 1-3 months who meet ALL low-risk criteria; under 8 weeks LP is mandatory and empirical antibiotics should not be withheld pending culture (RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence oral amoxicillin and arrange ED review at 24 hours
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are inadequate empirical cover for serious bacterial infection in a febrile infant under 3 months; IV therapy is required (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Perform a chest X-ray and observe for 4 hours without further investigations
options.rationale: CXR is not routinely indicated without respiratory signs; this does not substitute for blood, urine and CSF sampling in a febrile infant under 8 weeks (NICE NG143).

explanation.summary: Febrile infants under 8 weeks (some Australian centres extend to under 12 weeks) require full septic workup including CSF and empirical IV antibiotics regardless of low-risk biomarkers, because the Rochester, Philadelphia and Boston low-risk criteria miss approximately 1-3% of serious bacterial infections including meningitis.
key_points: Under 8 weeks: full septic workup including LP, empirical IV benzylpenicillin + gentamicin
8 weeks to 3 months: selective workup possible if well AND low-risk criteria met
Rochester low-risk: WCC 5-15, bands <1.5, urine WBC <10, stool WBC <5 if diarrhoea
CRP >20 and procalcitonin >0.5 increase risk of SBI
Listeria cover (amoxicillin/benzylpenicillin) required under 3 months
explanation.pearls: A well-looking febrile neonate is the most dangerous febrile patient in the department. Bacterial meningitis can present with normal WCC and CRP at this age.

sources.label: RCH Clinical Practice Guideline - Febrile child
Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Sepsis in neonates and infants

stem: A 3-year-old girl, weight 14 kg, presents to a regional ED at 02:30 with 18 hours of fever to 39.4 degC, reduced oral intake and increasing lethargy. She had a coryzal illness in the family last week. Past history includes mild eczema only and her vaccinations are up to date. On triage she is sleepy but rousable to voice, with mottled peripheries to the knees and elbows, cool hands and feet, a central capillary refill of 4 seconds, and a non-blanching petechial rash spreading on the trunk. She has not passed urine since the previous evening. Temperature is 39.1 degC, heart rate 178 bpm, respiratory rate 44, SpO2 96% on room air, and non-invasive BP is 92/58 mmHg. She does not localise to pain on abdominal examination. The nurse has activated a Paediatric Early Warning Score escalation and IV access has been obtained.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.1 degC, HR 178 bpm, RR 44, SpO2 96% RA, BP 92/58, cap refill 4 s.
data_table.Examination: Sleepy, rousable. Mottled to elbows/knees. Non-blanching petechial rash trunk. No neck stiffness elicited.
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.28, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, glucose 4.1 mmol/L, BE -7.

lead_in: Which set of interventions best meets the paediatric sepsis 1-hour bundle?

options.text: IV adrenaline infusion at 0.1 microg/kg/min as first intervention
options.rationale: Inotropes are indicated for fluid-refractory shock after 40-60 mL/kg of crystalloid, not as first-line before fluid resuscitation (ANZICS; APLS).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin, 10 mL/kg saline bolus, urgent CT head before lumbar puncture
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are inadequate in shock; CT head is not required before treatment and antibiotics must not be delayed for imaging or LP (ANZICS).

options.text: Blood cultures, IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg, 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus, lactate, reassess after first bolus
options.rationale: The Australian paediatric sepsis pathway requires recognition, IV/IO access, blood cultures before antibiotics where this does not delay, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 hour (ceftriaxone covers meningococcaemia which is the likely diagnosis given non-blanching rash and shock), a 10-20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid bolus with reassessment, and lactate measurement - all within the first hour (ANZICS Paediatric Sepsis Pathway; RCH CPG Sepsis).

options.text: Paracetamol 15 mg/kg PO and observation pending senior review in the morning
options.rationale: This is meningococcal septic shock until proven otherwise; antipyretic and observation alone is dangerous and breaches the 1-hour bundle (ANZICS).

options.text: Lumbar puncture first, then antibiotics, then fluid bolus
options.rationale: LP is contraindicated in shock and signs of raised ICP; antibiotics must come first and not be delayed for CSF sampling (RCH CPG Meningitis).

explanation.summary: Recognise paediatric sepsis from tachycardia disproportionate to fever, altered mental status, prolonged capillary refill and mottling - hypotension is a late sign. Deliver the 1-hour bundle: oxygen, IV/IO access, blood cultures, broad-spectrum IV antibiotic, 10-20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid bolus with reassessment after each, lactate, and senior/PIPER escalation.
key_points: Cold shock: cool peripheries, prolonged CR, narrow pulse pressure - common in children
Warm shock: bounding pulses, flash CR, wide pulse pressure - older children/adolescents
Antibiotic within 1 hour; do not delay for LP or imaging
Reassess after each 10-20 mL/kg bolus; watch for fluid overload (hepatomegaly, crackles)
Escalate to PIPER/NETS for transfer to PICU
explanation.pearls: A child with sepsis decompensates suddenly because they compensate well right up to the cliff edge. Trust prolonged capillary refill and altered mentation over a normal BP.

stem: An 18-month-old boy, weight 11 kg (weighed in clinic 2 weeks ago at 11.5 kg), is brought to ED with 36 hours of profuse non-bloody diarrhoea and three episodes of vomiting today. His older sibling had similar symptoms last week. He has been drinking some water but refuses solids. On examination he is irritable but consolable, with sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes, a slightly delayed skin recoil, capillary refill of 2-3 seconds centrally, warm peripheries, and tear production when crying. His nappy at triage was dry, the last wet nappy was 6 hours ago. HR 142, RR 28, T 37.4 degC, BP 96/60. There is no abdominal mass and no peritonism.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 degC, HR 142 bpm, RR 28, BP 96/60, cap refill 2-3 s.
data_table.Examination: Irritable. Sunken eyes, dry mucosae. Tears present. Skin recoil mildly delayed. Warm peripheries.
data_table.Investigations: Weight 11 kg (4% below recent baseline 11.5 kg). Urine output reduced.

lead_in: Which is the best estimate of the degree of dehydration?

options.text: No clinically detectable dehydration (<3%)
options.rationale: Multiple Gorelick signs are present (sunken eyes, dry mucosae, reduced urine output, abnormal skin recoil) so dehydration is clinically detectable (RCH CPG Gastroenteritis).

options.text: Mild to moderate dehydration (approximately 4-6%)
options.rationale: The child has 3-5 Gorelick signs (sunken eyes, dry mucosae, abnormal skin recoil, reduced urine output, mildly prolonged CR) with preserved consciousness, tears and BP, consistent with mild-to-moderate dehydration around 4-6%; this matches the weight deficit (0.5 kg of 11.5 kg = 4.3%) (RCH CPG Gastroenteritis).

options.text: Severe dehydration (>=10%) with compensated shock
options.rationale: Severe dehydration requires features such as lethargy/obtunded mental state, very prolonged CR >3 s, weak pulses, deeply sunken eyes and absent tears - this child does not meet that threshold (RCH CPG; WHO).

options.text: Hypovolaemic shock
options.rationale: BP, mental status and central perfusion are preserved; this is not decompensated shock (APLS).

options.text: Euvolaemic with isolated viral illness, no rehydration required
options.rationale: Clinical signs and weight loss demonstrate a deficit; rehydration is required (RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Use the Gorelick 10-point or WHO 4-sign assessment together with acute weight loss. Mild-moderate dehydration (3-7%) has 2-3 signs and preserved consciousness, tears and BP. Severe (>=10%) has obtunded mental state, deeply sunken eyes, absent tears, very prolonged CR and weak pulses.
key_points: Best single estimate of deficit: acute weight change
Most useful clinical signs: prolonged CR, abnormal skin turgor, abnormal respiratory pattern
BP is a late sign in children - compensated shock can have a normal BP
WHO: no/some/severe dehydration plans A/B/C
Gorelick >=3 signs = >5% dehydration
explanation.pearls: If the child is alert, drinking, and producing tears, severe dehydration is unlikely no matter how dramatic the diarrhoea history.

stem: A 2-year-old girl, weight 12 kg, presents with 48 hours of viral gastroenteritis (vomiting and watery diarrhoea, no blood). She is assessed in ED as approximately 5-6% dehydrated: dry mucosae, sunken eyes, capillary refill 2 s, reduced urine output. She is alert and accepts sips of water but vomits a 30 mL bolus after 5 minutes. She has no ileus, no abdominal distension and no surgical concerns. Electrolytes are not yet available. She has no significant comorbidity, no allergies and weighs near her plotted growth centile. The ED is busy and IV cannulation has been attempted unsuccessfully once. Her parents are willing to assist with whatever route is recommended.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 degC, HR 138, RR 28, BP 98/62, cap refill 2 s.
data_table.Examination: Alert, irritable. Dry mucosae. Soft abdomen, no distension, no peritonism. Bowel sounds active.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 4.4 mmol/L. No electrolytes yet.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate rehydration approach?

options.text: IV 0.9% saline 20 mL/kg bolus then maintenance plus deficit IV over 24 hours
options.rationale: IV rehydration is reserved for shock, severe dehydration, failed enteral rehydration, ileus or altered consciousness; first-line for mild-moderate dehydration is enteral (ORS or NG) (RCH CPG Gastroenteritis).

options.text: IV 0.45% saline with 5% dextrose at maintenance only, no bolus
options.rationale: 0.45% saline is not recommended as a routine paediatric IV fluid in Australia (risk of hyponatraemia); isotonic fluids are standard if the IV route is required (NHMRC paediatric IV fluids; RCH CPG IV fluids).

options.text: Encourage water and dilute apple juice ad libitum at home with GP review in 24 hours
options.rationale: Plain water lacks sodium and risks hyponatraemia in ongoing losses; ORS is preferred and ED-based rehydration is required given failed oral intake (RCH CPG).

options.text: Nasogastric rehydration with oral rehydration solution at 25 mL/kg/hour for 4 hours, then reassess
options.rationale: Rapid NG rehydration with ORS (Gastrolyte) at 25 mL/kg/h for 4 hours is the RCH first-line approach for mild-moderate dehydration in children who cannot tolerate oral sips, avoiding the risks of IV access and being non-inferior to IV for time to discharge and complications (RCH CPG Gastroenteritis; PREDICT NG/IV trial).

options.text: Ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg IV then discharge without rehydration
options.rationale: Ondansetron is a useful adjunct to enable oral rehydration but does not replace it; discharging without ensuring rehydration is inadequate (RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Mild-moderate dehydration (3-7%) is first managed enterally. Trial oral ORS in small frequent volumes (e.g., 5 mL every 1-2 minutes); if vomiting, give ondansetron (>=6 months) and re-trial, or switch to rapid NG rehydration at 25 mL/kg/h for 4 hours then reassess. IV is reserved for shock, severe dehydration, ileus, altered consciousness or failed enteral rehydration. Use isotonic fluids if IV is required.
key_points: Enteral first: oral then NG; both better tolerated than IV and equally effective in mild-moderate
Rapid NG: 25 mL/kg/h ORS for 4 hours = 100 mL/kg replacement
Ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg PO/IV reduces vomiting; one dose only
IV bolus 10-20 mL/kg 0.9% saline for shock; isotonic maintenance
Avoid hypotonic IV fluids - risk of hyponatraemic encephalopathy
explanation.pearls: An NG tube placed for rehydration is rarely refused once parents understand it avoids repeat cannulation attempts.

sources.label: PREDICT NG vs IV rehydration trial

stem: A 5-year-old boy, weight 20 kg, is brought to a school sick bay 10 minutes after eating a peanut butter sandwich at lunchtime, having previously only had a mild rash with peanut at age 2. Staff describe him as flushed with widespread urticaria on his chest and arms, persistent coughing with audible wheeze, and a hoarse voice. He is anxious but responsive, sitting forward. The school nurse measures HR 138, RR 38, SpO2 94% on room air. There is no stridor at rest but his voice sounds husky and he reports throat tightness. He has no history of asthma. His EpiPen Junior 150 microg is in the school office and an ambulance has been called.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 degC, HR 138 bpm, RR 38, SpO2 94% RA.
data_table.Examination: Flushed, widespread urticaria. Hoarse voice, throat tightness, audible wheeze. Sitting forward. No stridor at rest.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Administer IM adrenaline 150 microg (EpiPen Junior) into the anterolateral mid-thigh and lay flat with legs elevated
options.rationale: This meets ASCIA criteria for anaphylaxis (skin + respiratory involvement with airway compromise) and IM adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.5 mg) is first-line and must not be delayed. For 20 kg the 150 microg autoinjector is correct (10-20 kg use 150 microg). Position supine with legs elevated unless respiratory distress dictates sitting (ASCIA Guidelines for acute management of anaphylaxis).
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for acute management of anaphylaxis

options.text: Administer salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer and observe for response before adrenaline
options.rationale: Wheeze in anaphylaxis is treated with adrenaline first; bronchodilators are adjuncts. Delaying adrenaline increases mortality (ASCIA).
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for acute management of anaphylaxis

options.text: Administer oral cetirizine and prednisolone and observe for 1 hour
options.rationale: Antihistamines and corticosteroids are not first-line for anaphylaxis and do not treat airway or cardiovascular compromise; adrenaline is the only first-line drug (ASCIA).
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for acute management of anaphylaxis

options.text: Administer IM adrenaline 300 microg (EpiPen) into the anterolateral mid-thigh
options.rationale: 300 microg autoinjector is for >=20 kg in some protocols (>20 kg in ASCIA action plan); at 20 kg the 150 microg device is correct per ASCIA paediatric action plan thresholds (10-20 kg = 150 microg; >20 kg = 300 microg). 20 kg is the upper boundary and the smaller device is appropriate.

options.text: Withhold adrenaline as the child is talking and responsive; await paramedic arrival
options.rationale: Anaphylaxis with airway compromise (hoarse voice, throat tightness) requires immediate IM adrenaline; waiting risks deterioration before paramedic arrival (ASCIA).
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for acute management of anaphylaxis

explanation.summary: Anaphylaxis (ASCIA): any acute onset illness with typical skin features PLUS involvement of respiratory and/or cardiovascular and/or persistent severe GI symptoms; or acute onset hypotension/bronchospasm/upper airway obstruction even without skin signs. Treat with IM adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.5 mg) anterolateral mid-thigh, repeat every 5 minutes as needed.
key_points: Adrenaline IM is the only first-line drug
Autoinjector dosing: <7.5 kg consider IM ampoule, 7.5-20 kg = 150 microg, >20 kg = 300 microg
Positioning: supine + legs elevated unless airway compromise or vomiting
Salbutamol, oxygen, IV fluids are adjuncts
Antihistamines and steroids are NOT first-line and do not prevent biphasic reactions
explanation.pearls: If you are debating whether it is anaphylaxis, give the adrenaline. Underuse causes deaths; overuse causes transient palpitations.

sources.label: ASCIA Guidelines for acute management of anaphylaxis

subtopic: Refractory anaphylaxis after two IM doses
stem: A 10-year-old boy, weight 32 kg, presents to ED 25 minutes after a wasp sting with urticaria, lip swelling, stridor, expiratory wheeze and persistent hypotension. Paramedics gave IM adrenaline 300 microg en route. On arrival to ED 10 minutes later he remained tachypnoeic with stridor, wheeze and BP 78/40, so a second IM adrenaline 300 microg was given into the anterolateral thigh and an IV 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus was commenced. Now 5 minutes after the second IM dose he remains drowsy with BP 82/44, HR 156, RR 40, SpO2 92% on 15 L/min via non-rebreather, with audible stridor and wheeze. Both IM doses were confirmed delivered correctly. The ED consultant is at the bedside, anaesthetics has been called for airway support, and a second IV line has been sited.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 82/44, HR 156, RR 40, SpO2 92% on 15 L NRB.
data_table.Examination: Drowsy. Stridor and expiratory wheeze. Lip and tongue swelling. Mottled peripheries.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Bolus IV adrenaline 1 mg (1:10,000) over 1 minute
options.rationale: A 1 mg IV bolus is the cardiac arrest dose and would likely cause hypertensive crisis, myocardial ischaemia or arrhythmia in a perfusing patient; infusion is preferred (ASCIA).

options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg and IV promethazine 0.25 mg/kg
options.rationale: Corticosteroids and antihistamines do not treat refractory anaphylaxis and promethazine is contraindicated under 2 years and can worsen hypotension; adrenaline infusion is required (ASCIA).

options.text: Commence IV adrenaline infusion at 0.1 microg/kg/min, titrated to response
options.rationale: Refractory anaphylaxis after two correctly delivered IM doses is an indication for IV adrenaline infusion, starting at 0.1 microg/kg/min (range 0.05-1 microg/kg/min) and titrated to BP, perfusion and airway response, while continuing fluid resuscitation. Boluses of IV adrenaline outside cardiac arrest carry significant risk of arrhythmia and hypertensive crisis and are not first-line (ASCIA Refractory Anaphylaxis Guidelines).

options.text: Continue IM adrenaline every 5 minutes only and avoid IV adrenaline
options.rationale: After two failed IM doses, IM absorption is unreliable in shock; IV infusion is indicated (ASCIA Refractory Guidelines).

options.text: Commence IV glucagon 30 microg/kg as first-line, no further adrenaline
options.rationale: Glucagon is considered only in patients on beta-blockers with refractory shock; it does not replace adrenaline infusion (ASCIA).

explanation.summary: Refractory anaphylaxis = ongoing features after 2 appropriate IM adrenaline doses. Move to IV adrenaline infusion (0.1 microg/kg/min, titrate), aggressive IV fluids (up to 50 mL/kg crystalloid), prepare for early intubation given airway oedema, consider nebulised adrenaline for upper airway and salbutamol for bronchospasm. Glucagon if on beta-blockers; vasopressin if catecholamine-refractory.
key_points: IV adrenaline infusion: 0.1 microg/kg/min start, titrate 0.05-1
Continue fluid resuscitation 20 mL/kg, repeat to 50 mL/kg
Early airway control - intubation before complete obstruction
Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg for upper airway oedema
Salbutamol for persistent bronchospasm
explanation.pearls: If two correctly placed IM doses have failed, the problem is absorption or severity - not the drug. Move to infusion and call ICU.

stem: A 4-year-old girl, weight 16 kg, is brought into ED by ambulance with a generalised tonic-clonic seizure that began at home 12 minutes ago. Paramedics gave buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg 8 minutes after onset, but the seizure has continued. On arrival she is convulsing, with intact airway, no cyanosis, SpO2 96% on 8 L Hudson mask, HR 168, BP 110/70, T 38.1 degC. Bedside glucose is 5.8 mmol/L. She has no known epilepsy, no medications, and no significant past medical history. IV access has just been obtained.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.1 degC, HR 168, RR breaths irregular, SpO2 96% on 8 L Hudson, BP 110/70.
data_table.Examination: Generalised tonic-clonic activity ongoing. Airway patent with jaw thrust. No focal lateralising features evident.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 5.8 mmol/L. No bloods yet.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next medication?

options.text: Repeat buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg
options.rationale: Now that IV access is available the IV route is preferred for the second benzodiazepine dose because of more reliable absorption (RCH CPG).

options.text: IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg over 20 minutes
options.rationale: Phenytoin (or levetiracetam) is the third-line agent after two doses of benzodiazepine have failed; only one benzodiazepine has been given so far (RCH CPG).

options.text: IV phenobarbital 20 mg/kg
options.rationale: Phenobarbital is reserved for refractory status after benzodiazepines and a second-line agent have failed; it is not the next step (APLS).

options.text: Rapid sequence intubation with thiopentone and rocuronium
options.rationale: RSI and induction of coma is reserved for refractory status after benzodiazepines and at least one second-line anticonvulsant have failed; airway is currently patent and oxygenation maintained (APLS).

options.text: IV midazolam 0.15 mg/kg
options.rationale: Per APLS Australia and RCH status epilepticus pathway, after one benzodiazepine that has failed (and 10 minutes have elapsed), the second-line is a SECOND benzodiazepine dose - IV midazolam 0.15 mg/kg (or IV diazepam 0.25 mg/kg) - now that IV access is available. If that fails after a further 10 minutes, move to a second-line agent such as levetiracetam 40 mg/kg or phenytoin 20 mg/kg (RCH CPG; APLS).

explanation.summary: APLS status epilepticus pathway: 0 min - ABC, oxygen, check glucose. 5 min - first benzodiazepine (IV midazolam/diazepam or buccal/IM midazolam). 15 min - second benzodiazepine. 25 min - second-line: levetiracetam 40-60 mg/kg IV over 5 min OR phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV over 20 min. 45 min - RSI with thiopentone, transfer to PICU.
key_points: First and second benzodiazepine before moving to long-acting agent
Levetiracetam now preferred over phenytoin in many centres (faster, fewer arrhythmias, EcLiPSE/ConSEPT trials)
Check and correct glucose, sodium, calcium, magnesium
Pyridoxine 100 mg IV if under 18 months and refractory
Get help early - status >30 minutes risks neurological injury
explanation.pearls: Most paediatric status responds to two doses of benzodiazepine. If you are about to give a third benzodiazepine, you are wrong - move on.

subtopic: First non-febrile seizure - investigations and counselling
stem: A previously well 5-year-old boy, weight 19 kg, is brought to ED after a witnessed event at home in which he became suddenly unresponsive, stiffened, then had rhythmic jerking of all four limbs for about 90 seconds, followed by 10 minutes of confusion and sleepiness. He has now fully recovered. He has had no fever, no head injury, no toxin exposure, no recent illness, and no family history of epilepsy. Birth and developmental history are normal and he is fully immunised. On examination he is alert, neurologically intact with no focal deficit, no rash, no signs of infection, normal fundi, and a normal cardiovascular exam. Bedside glucose is 4.9 mmol/L. Sodium, calcium and magnesium are normal. He has no further seizures during 4 hours of ED observation.

data_table.Examination: GCS 15. Normal cranial nerves, tone, power, reflexes, coordination, gait. No rash. No meningism.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 4.9, Na 139, K 4.1, Ca 2.35, Mg 0.85, FBC normal, CRP <5.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan?

options.text: Discharge home with outpatient EEG within 1-2 weeks, MRI only if focal features develop, safety advice, no anti-epileptic medication, no driving restrictions for parents
options.rationale: After a first unprovoked generalised tonic-clonic seizure in a neurologically normal child, the standard approach is outpatient first-fit clinic review with EEG within 1-2 weeks (which informs recurrence risk), MRI only if there are focal features or under 2 years or refractory, no anti-epileptic medication after a single seizure unless recurrence risk is high, and safety counselling (no baths alone, supervised swimming, no high climbing). Parental driving is not restricted (RCH CPG Afebrile seizures).

options.text: Admit and commence sodium valproate 20 mg/kg/day
options.rationale: Anti-epileptic medication is generally not started after a single unprovoked seizure; recurrence is approximately 40% and treatment after the first seizure does not change long-term prognosis (RCH; NICE).

options.text: Urgent inpatient CT head and lumbar puncture before discharge
options.rationale: CT and LP are not indicated after a brief generalised seizure with full neurological recovery, no fever, no trauma and no meningism (RCH CPG).

options.text: Discharge with a buccal midazolam rescue kit and no follow-up unless further seizures
options.rationale: First-fit follow-up with EEG is the standard of care to characterise the seizure type and recurrence risk; a rescue kit is not routinely given after a single brief seizure (RCH CPG).

options.text: Discharge with same-day MRI brain and start levetiracetam if MRI abnormal
options.rationale: Urgent MRI is not required for a generalised seizure with normal neurology; outpatient imaging is reserved for focal features, persistent deficit, refractory seizures or under 2 years (RCH).

explanation.summary: First unprovoked afebrile seizure in an otherwise well child: ABC, exclude provokers (glucose, electrolytes, toxins, infection, trauma), full neurological examination. If recovered and normal: discharge with first-fit clinic referral, EEG within 1-2 weeks, MRI only for focal features/under 2/refractory, safety advice, no AED. Recurrence risk approximately 40%; epilepsy is diagnosed after 2 unprovoked seizures.
key_points: EEG within 1-2 weeks of first seizure characterises syndrome
MRI for: focal seizure, under 2 years, persistent deficit, refractory
AEDs not started after single seizure (no change in long-term outcome)
Safety: baths supervised, no swimming alone, no high climbing
Australian driving rules apply to the patient (>16) not the parents
explanation.pearls: First-fit clinics exist for a reason - rushing to imaging and starting medication after one event is overinvestigation and overtreatment.

stem: A previously well 18-month-old boy, weight 11 kg, presented to ED after a single 3-minute generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home. He had been febrile to 39.0 degC with coryzal symptoms for the past 24 hours. He recovered fully within 20 minutes. On examination he is alert, playful, has a clear viral URTI and no other source of infection. There is no rash, no meningism, and no focal neurological signs. He has no past seizure history; his father had two febrile seizures as a toddler. Vaccinations are up to date.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7 degC, HR 138, RR 28, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Alert, playful. Coryza. No rash, no meningism, no focal signs. Anterior fontanelle closed.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 5.2 mmol/L. No further investigations performed.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan?

options.text: Admit for EEG and MRI
options.rationale: EEG and MRI are not indicated for simple febrile seizures (RCH CPG).

options.text: Lumbar puncture before discharge
options.rationale: LP is not routinely indicated for a simple febrile seizure in a fully recovered, alert child without meningism (RCH CPG).

options.text: Start regular paracetamol and ibuprofen alternating to prevent recurrence
options.rationale: Antipyretics do not prevent febrile seizure recurrence; they are used only for comfort (RCH CPG).

options.text: Reassurance, safety advice, paracetamol for comfort only, discharge home with GP follow-up
options.rationale: This is a simple febrile seizure (single, generalised, <15 minutes, full recovery, no recurrence in 24 hours, age 6 months to 5 years). Management is reassurance and parent education; no investigations are needed if a clear source is found, no anti-epileptic is indicated, and aggressive antipyresis does not prevent recurrence. Risk of subsequent epilepsy is approximately 1-2% (slightly above baseline) (RCH CPG Febrile seizures).

options.text: Prescribe maintenance sodium valproate
options.rationale: Maintenance anti-epileptic medication is not indicated for simple febrile seizures (RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Simple febrile seizure: age 6 months to 5 years, generalised, <15 minutes, single in 24 hours, full recovery, with fever and no CNS infection. Management: identify and treat the febrile illness, reassurance, safety advice, no investigations beyond what the source illness requires, no anti-epileptic, antipyretics for comfort only.
key_points: Complex = focal OR >15 min OR >1 in 24 h
Recurrence risk approximately 30% overall; higher if first seizure <18 months or family history
Subsequent epilepsy risk approximately 1-2% (background 0.5-1%)
Antipyretics do NOT prevent recurrence
LP only if meningism, age <6 months, or incomplete recovery
explanation.pearls: Parents are usually traumatised by their first febrile seizure - the most therapeutic intervention is unhurried explanation that this is benign.

stem: A 4-year-old boy, weight 18 kg, presents with 3 days of high fever and a 1-day history of vomiting and reduced intake. He has no chronic illness. On examination he is irritable but rousable to voice, tachycardic, with mottled knees, cool hands and feet, central capillary refill of 4 seconds, peripheral pulses present but thready, and a non-tender abdomen with no rash. His HR is 168, RR 36, T 39.4 degC, SpO2 97% on room air, and his non-invasive BP is 96/64 mmHg. He has not voided in 8 hours. Bedside glucose is 4.4 mmol/L, venous lactate 3.6 mmol/L. IV access is being established.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 degC, HR 168, RR 36, BP 96/64, SpO2 97% RA, cap refill 4 s.
data_table.Examination: Irritable, rousable. Mottled, cool peripheries. Thready pulses. Soft abdomen. No rash.

lead_in: Which best describes his haemodynamic state and the immediate management?

options.text: Cardiogenic shock; restrict fluid and commence dobutamine
options.rationale: No history or examination findings (gallop, hepatomegaly, crackles) suggest cardiogenic aetiology; presentation is septic (APLS).

options.text: Decompensated shock; commence adrenaline infusion immediately before any fluid
options.rationale: BP is preserved so this is compensated shock; fluid resuscitation precedes inotropes, which are added if shock is fluid-refractory after 40-60 mL/kg (APLS).

options.text: No shock; give maintenance fluid only and observe
options.rationale: Cap refill 4 s, mottling, oliguria, lactate 3.6 and disproportionate tachycardia are not normal and indicate shock requiring resuscitation, not maintenance alone (APLS).

options.text: Warm shock; give noradrenaline infusion as first-line
options.rationale: Warm shock is characterised by flash CR, bounding pulses and wide pulse pressure; this child has the opposite picture (cold shock). Adrenaline is the typical first-line inotrope in paediatric cold shock if fluid-refractory (APLS).

options.text: Compensated cold shock; give 10-20 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus over 5-10 minutes and reassess
options.rationale: Tachycardia disproportionate to fever, prolonged capillary refill, cool mottled peripheries, oliguria and an elevated lactate define compensated cold shock with maintained BP. The first-line intervention is a 10-20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid bolus over 5-10 minutes with reassessment after each bolus, alongside broad-spectrum antibiotics if sepsis is suspected (APLS Australia; RCH CPG Sepsis).

explanation.summary: Children compensate well by raising heart rate and systemic vascular resistance; hypotension is a late and pre-arrest sign. Recognise compensated shock from tachycardia disproportionate to fever, prolonged capillary refill, mottling, cool peripheries, altered mental state, and oliguria, regardless of BP. Treat with 10-20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid boluses with reassessment after each.
key_points: Hypotension is a late sign in children - do not wait for it
Cold shock: cool peripheries, prolonged CR, narrow pulse pressure - first-line adrenaline if fluid-refractory
Warm shock: flash CR, wide pulse pressure - first-line noradrenaline if fluid-refractory
Bolus 10-20 mL/kg over 5-10 minutes; reassess after each
Stop fluid if signs of overload (hepatomegaly, crackles)
explanation.pearls: A normal BP in a sick child is reassurance you don't deserve. Look at the periphery, the lactate and the mental state.

stem: A 6-year-old girl, weight 22 kg, was brought in shocked with presumed septic source (community-acquired pneumonia with a parapneumonic effusion on bedside lung ultrasound). She received empirical IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg early in the resuscitation. Over the last 40 minutes she has received three 20 mL/kg boluses of 0.9% saline (total 60 mL/kg) and remains tachycardic at 156, with central capillary refill of 4 seconds, cool mottled peripheries, BP 78/40 mmHg, RR 38, SpO2 94% on 8 L Hudson mask. She has not voided since arrival. The hepatic edge is now palpable 1 cm below the costal margin and there are bibasal crackles. Lactate has fallen from 5.4 to 3.8 mmol/L. The retrieval service is on the phone and the paediatric registrar is preparing for central access. The child has no congenital heart disease.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 156, BP 78/40, RR 38, SpO2 94% on 8 L, cap refill 4 s.
data_table.Examination: Cool mottled peripheries, narrow pulse pressure. Hepatomegaly 1 cm. Bibasal crackles. Anuric.
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 3.8 mmol/L (was 5.4). VBG pH 7.28. CXR: right lower lobe consolidation with small effusion.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence peripheral adrenaline infusion at 0.05-0.1 microg/kg/min, hold further fluid boluses, and prepare for central access and intubation
options.rationale: She has fluid-refractory septic shock with cold-shock physiology (narrow pulse pressure, mottled cool peripheries, prolonged CR) and now signs of fluid overload (hepatomegaly, crackles). Australian guidelines support starting adrenaline as first-line inotrope in paediatric cold septic shock, and peripheral infusion at low-dose concentrations (0.05-0.3 microg/kg/min) is acceptable while central access is obtained. Further large-volume fluid boluses risk worsening overload (ANZICS Paediatric Sepsis; APLS; Surviving Sepsis Campaign paediatric 2020).

options.text: Give a fourth 20 mL/kg saline bolus before any vasoactive medication
options.rationale: With hepatomegaly and crackles signalling overload, further large boluses are likely to worsen oedema and hypoxaemia; inotrope is the next step (Surviving Sepsis paediatric 2020).

options.text: Start noradrenaline as first-line inotrope at 0.1 microg/kg/min
options.rationale: Noradrenaline is preferred in warm shock with wide pulse pressure and vasodilation; in cold shock with poor cardiac output, adrenaline is first-line in paediatric guidelines (APLS).

options.text: Commence dopamine 10 microg/kg/min peripherally
options.rationale: Dopamine has fallen out of favour as first-line in paediatric septic shock due to inferior outcomes vs adrenaline/noradrenaline (Ventura 2015 RCT; Surviving Sepsis paediatric 2020).

options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 2 mg/kg as the next intervention with no inotrope
options.rationale: Stress-dose hydrocortisone is an adjunct in catecholamine-resistant shock or suspected adrenal insufficiency, not a substitute for inotropic support in fluid-refractory shock (Surviving Sepsis paediatric 2020).

explanation.summary: Fluid-refractory shock after 40-60 mL/kg crystalloid requires vasoactive support. Choose adrenaline for cold shock (low cardiac output, high SVR) and noradrenaline for warm shock (high cardiac output, low SVR). Peripheral low-dose infusion is acceptable while central access is established. Watch for fluid overload (hepatomegaly, crackles, S3, increasing oxygen requirement) and stop boluses if present.
key_points: Fluid-refractory = persistent shock after 40-60 mL/kg
Cold shock -> adrenaline 0.05-0.3 microg/kg/min
Warm shock -> noradrenaline 0.05-0.3 microg/kg/min
Peripheral infusion safe at low concentrations until central access
Hydrocortisone 50 mg/m2/day or 2 mg/kg if catecholamine-resistant
explanation.pearls: If you can feel the liver, you can stop the fluid. Paediatric fluid overload is iatrogenic and worsens outcomes.

stem: A 4-month-old, ex-term, breastfed boy is brought to ED by his mother because of unsettled crying for the last 12 hours. She mentions that she noticed two bruises on his cheek and a bruise on his left ear earlier in the day, which she attributes to him knocking himself on a toy in the cot. He has not yet rolled or crawled and is not pulling himself up. There is no preceding illness and no fever. He is fully immunised and has had routine well-child checks with no concerns. Examination reveals a well-grown alert infant with a 2 cm bruise to the right cheek, a 1 cm bruise to the left pinna and a 1.5 cm bruise on the left frontal scalp, no other external injuries, a normal anterior fontanelle, no focal neurology, no retinal haemorrhages on examination by the registrar, normal tone, and a normal abdomen with no organomegaly. Coagulation screen, FBC and LFTs are normal. The mother is alone and tearful when asked about who else cares for the baby.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 degC, HR 142, RR 36, SpO2 99% RA.
data_table.Examination: Three bruises (cheek, ear, frontal scalp). Otherwise unremarkable. Pre-mobile.
data_table.Investigations: FBC, coags, LFTs normal. No CT or skeletal survey yet.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Send for outpatient coagulation studies only and review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Coags are already normal and a bleeding disorder does not explain pre-mobile bruising in suspicious patterns; comprehensive abuse workup and notification are required (RCH CPG).

options.text: Reassure the mother and discharge with GP follow-up in 1 week
options.rationale: Bruises in a pre-mobile infant are never normal - 'those who don't cruise rarely bruise'. Discharge without investigation and mandatory reporting is a serious safeguarding failure (RCH CPG).

options.text: Admit for skeletal survey, CT head, ophthalmology review for retinal haemorrhages, child protection consult and mandatory report to the relevant statutory child protection agency
options.rationale: Bruising in a pre-mobile infant meets the TEN-4 FACESp rule (Torso, Ear, Neck, age <=4 months; Frenulum, Angle of jaw, Cheek, Eyelid, Sclera, patterned) and is a strong red flag for inflicted injury. This child has all four red-flag locations triggered. Mandatory next steps are full skeletal survey (and repeat at 2 weeks), non-contrast CT head, dilated ophthalmology examination for retinal haemorrhages, paediatric team and child protection consultation, and mandatory notification to the state child protection authority. Doctors are mandated reporters in every Australian state and territory (RCH CPG Child abuse; AAP TEN-4 FACES).

options.text: Order a skeletal survey only, without notifying child protection, pending the imaging result
options.rationale: Mandatory reporting is triggered by reasonable suspicion, not by waiting for confirmatory imaging; delay risks further harm and breaches statutory duty (Children and Young Persons Acts; RCH CPG).

options.text: Confront the mother directly about possible abuse before doing any investigations
options.rationale: Confrontation is not the role of the treating clinician; the priority is the child's safety, the workup, multidisciplinary consultation and statutory notification (RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Bruising in a pre-mobile infant is the most specific sign of physical abuse in early childhood. Apply the TEN-4 FACESp rule: bruises in any of these regions or in a child <=4 months are highly suspicious. Investigations: full skeletal survey (with 2-week repeat), CT head, dilated fundus exam, coagulation screen. Mandatory reporting in all Australian jurisdictions for doctors with reasonable suspicion.
key_points: Those who don't cruise rarely bruise
TEN-4: Torso, Ear, Neck, age 4 months and under
FACESp: Frenulum, Angle of jaw, Cheek, Eyelid, Sclera, patterned
Skeletal survey at presentation and repeat at 2 weeks (occult fractures)
Mandatory reporting is by suspicion, not certainty - this is statutory
explanation.pearls: Document objectively, do not investigate the social history beyond what is clinically necessary, and let the multidisciplinary team and statutory agency lead the safeguarding process.

sources.label: Pierce et al. TEN-4 FACESp validation 2021
AIFS Mandatory reporting of child abuse

stem: A 4-year-old girl, weight 16 kg, was pulled from a backyard pool by her father approximately 6 minutes ago after an unwitnessed submersion estimated at 3-5 minutes. She was apnoeic and unresponsive at poolside. Her father began chest compressions immediately and bystander CPR continued until paramedics arrived 4 minutes ago. Paramedics found her in asystole, established a supraglottic airway, ventilated with 100% oxygen and continued compressions; ROSC was achieved after 3 minutes of resuscitation. She arrives in ED still intubated (now endotracheal), with HR 132, BP 86/52, SpO2 92% on 100% FiO2, T 33.4 degC tympanic, GCS 3 with no sedation, pupils 4 mm sluggish bilaterally. Bedside lung ultrasound shows bilateral B-lines and the CXR is in progress.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, BP 86/52, SpO2 92% on FiO2 1.0, T 33.4 degC, GCS 3.
data_table.Examination: Intubated and ventilated. Bilateral crackles. Cool peripheries. Pupils 4 mm, sluggish.
data_table.Investigations: Lung US: bilateral B-lines. VBG pending. Bedside glucose 7.1 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which best reflects current ANZCOR-aligned management priorities for this child?

options.text: Withdraw care now given asystole at scene; outcome is uniformly poor
options.rationale: Paediatric drowning with ROSC and short submersion time can have favourable neurological outcomes; early prognostication is unreliable and resuscitation should continue with PICU input (ANZCOR).

options.text: Hyperventilate to PaCO2 25 mmHg and target SpO2 100% on FiO2 1.0 for the first 24 hours
options.rationale: Hyperoxia and hypocapnia worsen post-arrest neurological outcomes through cerebral vasoconstriction and oxidative injury; target normoxia and normocapnia (ANZCOR).

options.text: Aggressive rapid rewarming to 38 degC and prophylactic IV ceftriaxone
options.rationale: Rapid rewarming and induced hyperthermia worsen neurological outcomes; antibiotics are reserved for evidence of infection or contaminated water exposure with specific organisms (ANZCOR).

options.text: Extubate as soon as possible because the lungs are clear on auscultation and the chest X-ray is unremarkable so far
options.rationale: Pulmonary oedema and ARDS from drowning can be delayed (up to 8 hours); the patient has bilateral B-lines and a low GCS, both contraindicating early extubation (ANZCOR).

options.text: Maintain ventilation with lung-protective settings and PEEP, target normoxia and normocapnia, allow controlled rewarming to 36 degC, treat as post-cardiac-arrest syndrome with hypotension supported by fluids and adrenaline, CXR for aspiration/ARDS, and discuss with PICU/ECMO retrieval given persistent hypoxia
options.rationale: Post-drowning care follows ANZCOR resuscitation principles plus post-cardiac-arrest care. Five initial rescue breaths precede compressions in drowning because hypoxia is the proximate cause. After ROSC, target normoxia (SpO2 94-98%), normocapnia, lung-protective ventilation with PEEP for pulmonary oedema/ARDS, treat shock with fluids and adrenaline, and discuss VA-ECMO for refractory hypoxia or cardiac dysfunction. Rewarm to 36 degC at 0.5-1 degC/h (do not over-rewarm; mild controlled hypothermia is no longer routinely targeted but normothermia is). Antibiotics are not routine.
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.2 Resuscitation of the drowning victim
ANZCOR Guideline 12 Paediatric advanced life support

explanation.summary: Drowning is primarily an asphyxial injury - prioritise oxygenation. Bystander BLS for drowning: 5 rescue breaths first, then 30:2 CPR (lay rescuer) or 15:2 (two trained rescuers in a child). Post-ROSC: lung-protective ventilation with PEEP for pulmonary oedema/ARDS, normoxia, normocapnia, treat shock, rewarm to 36 degC, manage as post-cardiac-arrest syndrome. Consider VA-ECMO for refractory hypoxia or cardiac dysfunction. Late ARDS can develop up to 8 hours later.
key_points: 5 rescue breaths first in drowning (not compressions first)
Target SpO2 94-98% post-ROSC; avoid hyperoxia and hypocapnia
PEEP for pulmonary oedema/ARDS; lung-protective tidal volumes
Rewarm to 36 degC at 0.5-1 degC/h; avoid hyperthermia
Antibiotics not routine; consider if contaminated water or sepsis develops
explanation.pearls: The lungs of a drowned child can look normal at 1 hour and ARDS by 8 hours. Do not extubate to room air based on the initial CXR.

sources.label: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.2 Resuscitation of the drowning victim
ANZCOR Guideline 12 Paediatric advanced life support

stem: A 3-year-old girl, weight 15 kg, sustained a scald injury 90 minutes ago when she pulled a pot of hot water onto herself. On assessment in ED, she has partial-thickness burns to the anterior chest, anterior abdomen and the front of both thighs, with sparing of the face, hands, perineum and circumferential injuries. Using a Lund-Browder chart for her age, the total burn surface area (TBSA) is calculated at 18%. She is alert, in pain (Faces score 8/10), HR 148, BP 96/60, RR 32, SpO2 99% on room air, T 36.4 degC. IV access has been obtained and tetanus status is up to date. She last ate 4 hours ago and has no known allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 148, BP 96/60, RR 32, SpO2 99% RA, T 36.4 degC.
data_table.Examination: Partial-thickness burns anterior chest, abdomen, anterior thighs. 18% TBSA. Mucosae moist. Peripheral perfusion adequate.
data_table.Investigations: VBG pending. No carboxyhaemoglobin elevation (no inhalation injury suspected).

lead_in: Which fluid prescription best matches Australian paediatric burns guidance for the first 24 hours?

options.text: 5% albumin 20 mL/kg bolus immediately then 1 mL/kg/%TBSA over the next 24 hours
options.rationale: Colloid is generally avoided in the first 24 hours of burn resuscitation due to capillary leak; crystalloid is standard (ANZBA EMSB).

options.text: Parkland: 4 mL/kg/%TBSA 0.9% saline = 1080 mL over 24 hours INSTEAD OF maintenance
options.rationale: Children require maintenance fluid in addition to burn resuscitation fluid (not instead of), and Hartmann's is preferred over 0.9% saline (RCH CPG Burns).

options.text: Hypertonic saline 3% at 3 mL/kg/h for 24 hours
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline is not first-line in paediatric burn resuscitation in Australian practice (RCH; ANZBA).

options.text: Parkland: 3 mL/kg/%TBSA Hartmann's = 810 mL over 24 hours, half in first 8 hours from time of injury, with maintenance dextrose-containing fluid given in addition
options.rationale: Australian paediatric burns centres use a modified Parkland formula at 3-4 mL/kg/%TBSA of Hartmann's (compound sodium lactate) over the first 24 hours from the time of burn, half in the first 8 hours and half in the next 16 hours, with paediatric maintenance fluid containing glucose given IN ADDITION because children have lower glycogen stores and are at risk of hypoglycaemia. 3 mL x 15 kg x 18 = 810 mL resuscitation fluid (RCH CPG Burns; ANZBA EMSB).
source_refs: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns (EMSB)

options.text: Maintenance fluid only (60 mL/h of 0.9% saline + 5% dextrose) with no additional burn resuscitation fluid
options.rationale: An 18% TBSA burn exceeds the 10% threshold above which paediatric burn resuscitation fluid is required in addition to maintenance (RCH CPG Burns).

explanation.summary: Paediatric burns >=10% TBSA need formal resuscitation. Use modified Parkland: 3-4 mL/kg/%TBSA Hartmann's over 24 hours from time of burn, half in first 8 h, half in next 16 h. Give maintenance dextrose-containing fluid IN ADDITION (children risk hypoglycaemia). Use Lund-Browder chart (not rule of 9s) for paediatric TBSA. Titrate fluid to urine output 1 mL/kg/h. Analgesia (IV morphine or intranasal fentanyl), tetanus, cover with cling film, refer to burns service.
key_points: Modified Parkland 3-4 mL/kg/%TBSA Hartmann's over 24 h
Maintenance with glucose IN ADDITION in children
Lund-Browder chart for TBSA in children
Target urine output 1 mL/kg/h
Refer >10% TBSA, face/hands/perineum, full-thickness, electrical, chemical, suspected NAI
explanation.pearls: Children dehydrate and become hypoglycaemic faster than adults - never run a burned child without glucose-containing maintenance.

sources.label: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns (EMSB)

stem: A 15-year-old girl, weight 60 kg, is brought to ED by her mother 5 hours after ingesting an unknown number of paracetamol 500 mg tablets in an acute single ingestion. She is now tearful but cooperative, denies co-ingestion, has no abdominal pain, no jaundice, and a soft non-tender abdomen. She has no chronic liver disease and is not on enzyme-inducing medications. Vital signs are normal. Bloods drawn 4 hours and 30 minutes post-ingestion show paracetamol concentration 165 mg/L, ALT 28 U/L, INR 1.0, creatinine 58 micromol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L, glucose 5.0 mmol/L, and a negative beta-hCG. The empty packet retrieved at home suggests she may have taken approximately 14 g (around 230 mg/kg) but the ingestion history is uncertain. The Australian paracetamol nomogram has a single treatment line starting at 150 mg/L at 4 hours, dropping to 75 mg/L at 8 hours and 37 mg/L at 12 hours, after which the line is extended to 24 hours.

data_table.Examination: Alert, tearful. No jaundice. Soft non-tender abdomen.
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol 165 mg/L at 4.5 h. ALT 28. INR 1.0. Creatinine 58. Bicarbonate 24. Lactate 1.4. Glucose 5.0. beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat paracetamol level at 8 hours and only commence NAC if the 8-hour level is above 75 mg/L
options.rationale: If the 4-hour-equivalent level is already above the treatment line, NAC should be commenced immediately rather than waiting; delaying NAC beyond 8 hours significantly reduces its hepatoprotective efficacy (Australian PIC).
source_refs: Australian Poisons Information Centre paracetamol guideline

options.text: Commence IV N-acetylcysteine using the two-bag regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg over 16 h) and admit; arrange mental health review
options.rationale: At 4.5 hours post-ingestion the paracetamol concentration of 165 mg/L lies above the Australian 150 mg/L treatment line on the modified Rumack-Matthew nomogram (extrapolated back to 4 h would be above 150 mg/L). The reported dose also exceeds the 200 mg/kg supratherapeutic threshold. NAC is indicated. The Australian Poisons Information Centre and AMH endorse the two-bag regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg over 16 h) which reduces anaphylactoid reactions versus the traditional three-bag regimen. Psychiatric assessment is required after any deliberate self-poisoning (AMH; Australian PIC; Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology and Wilderness).
source_refs: Australian Poisons Information Centre paracetamol guideline

options.text: Give activated charcoal 1 g/kg orally now and reassess at 12 hours
options.rationale: Activated charcoal is effective within 1-2 hours of ingestion (up to 4 hours for sustained release); at 5 hours post-ingestion it is unlikely to be useful, and NAC should not be delayed (Australian PIC).
source_refs: Australian Poisons Information Centre paracetamol guideline

options.text: Discharge with mental health follow-up; the level is below the treatment line and the ingestion was less than 200 mg/kg
options.rationale: 165 mg/L at 4.5 hours is above the 150 mg/L treatment line, and the reported dose of approximately 230 mg/kg exceeds 200 mg/kg; NAC is indicated. Even if there is uncertainty, the higher of the two assessment methods (level vs dose) directs management (Australian PIC).
source_refs: Australian Poisons Information Centre paracetamol guideline

options.text: Commence oral methionine and arrange liver transplant assessment now
options.rationale: IV NAC is the standard antidote in Australia; oral methionine is not used. Transplant assessment is reserved for established fulminant hepatic failure (King's College criteria) which is not present here (AMH).

explanation.summary: Acute single paracetamol ingestion: take a 4-hour-or-later level and plot on the Australian nomogram with a single treatment line starting at 150 mg/L at 4 h. NAC indicated if level above line, OR dose >200 mg/kg (or >10 g) with no level available within 8 hours, OR staggered/repeated supratherapeutic ingestion, OR ALT elevated. Use the two-bag IV NAC regimen: 200 mg/kg over 4 h then 100 mg/kg over 16 h. After deliberate self-poisoning, mental health assessment is mandatory.
key_points: Australian nomogram: single 150 mg/L line at 4 h, log-linear to 24 h
Two-bag NAC: 200 mg/kg over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg over 16 h (fewer reactions vs 3-bag)
Supratherapeutic: >200 mg/kg or >10 g acute
Charcoal useful only within 1-2 h (up to 4 h for SR)
Repeat ALT, INR, paracetamol at end of NAC; continue NAC if ALT rising or INR >1.3
explanation.pearls: Never withhold NAC waiting for a perfect history. When dose, level and time are uncertain, treat - hepatotoxicity is the unrecoverable error.

sources.label: Australian Poisons Information Centre - Paracetamol poisoning guideline (Chiew et al. 2020)
